ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish tikka; closest parent 'fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific tikka preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Fish dish (food)' is the closest parent; no specific tikka or tandoori fish code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Machil Tikka consists of approximately 150 g of boneless grilled fish pieces, usually presented on a platter with sliced onions, lemon wedges, and mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Fish tikka / Tandoori fish — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15264)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for tandoori fish / fish tikka preparations. USDA SR Legacy fish (dry heat cooked) used to cross-validate protein and fat values. Marinade contribution (yoghurt, spices) is minor and consistent with ICMR-NIN composite values. Energy calculated from macros: protein 22.5 g × 4 + fat 5.8 g × 9 + carbohydrate 3.2 g × 4 ≈ 153 kcal per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled fish preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on grilled/baked fish preparations (GI ~30, low category) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The marinade (yoghurt, cumin, garam masala) contributes minimal carbohydrate and does not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 3.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) from marinade spices and yoghurt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size, as a 150 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium (~25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced salt in the marinade is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Machil Tikka is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a standard 150 g serving delivers approximately 4.8 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~1.4, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High-quality lean fish protein with beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled fish tikka provides approximately 1.9 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g including omega-3 fatty acids, and is prepared without deep-frying, making it a heart-healthy protein option when consumed as part of a balanced diet; sodium intake should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Machil Tikka (also spelled Machli Tikka or Machi Tikka) is a classic North Indian grilled fish dish in which boneless chunks of fish are marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing cumin, garam masala, and other aromatic spices, then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a dish with a lightly charred, smoky exterior and a moist, tender interior, carrying the warm, earthy flavours of the spice blend. In Singapore, it is served at North Indian restaurants such as Bawarchi on South Bridge Road, typically accompanied by sliced raw onions, lemon wedges, and mint-coriander chutney. Nutritionally, Machil Tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 22.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a low glycemic index of approximately 30. It is rich in vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, making it beneficial for cardiovascular health. The dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its minimal glycemic impact, and is generally appropriate for those following halal and gluten-free diets. Sodium content from the marinade warrants attention for individuals with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is machli tikka good for diabetics?
How many calories in fish tikka?
What fish is used in machil tikka?
What is the GI of fish tikka?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for tandoori fish / fish tikka preparations and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy grilled fish data. No exact entry for 'Machil Tikka' as a branded restaurant dish exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for grilled fish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food name normalised from 'Machil Tikka (Chef Recommendation)' to canonical 'Machil Tikka'; the parenthetical 'Chef Recommendation' is a menu descriptor, not part of the food name. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) tandoori fish composite and USDA SR Legacy (NDB 15264) grilled fish; exact restaurant recipe is unknown. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a minimally processed grilled fish preparation with no hydrogenated fats; this is naturally zero for this food type. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on grilled fish preparations in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct measurement for fish tikka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.9), potassium_mg (420.0), calcium_mg (38.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (68.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (4.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.35), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.8), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (4.5), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (240.0), magnesium_mg (32.0), zinc_mg (0.7), selenium_mcg (28.0), water_g (67.0) are all estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy composite values for grilled fish; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates are available from approved sources. 6. This dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (field survey source GD003649). 7. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided.

Added all mandatory Singaporean-dish multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated per_100g from ICMR-NIN (2017) tandoori fish composite and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15264 grilled fish.
Set GI to 30 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as naturally zero for grilled fish with no hydrogenated fats.
Provided all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
